# Expectations {#sec-expectations}

## Work environment

Our group includes a mix of RAs, PhD students, postdocs, and visiting researchers. The degree of in-person presence expected varies by role:

- **Research staff (RAs, data scientists):** Generally expected to be available during regular business hours. Remote work is fine, but coordinate with your project team so people know how to reach you.
- **PhD students:** Your schedule is your own. That said, being present for lab meetings, seminars, and collaborative work sessions is important.
- **Postdocs and visitors:** Discuss a schedule with Tamma that works for both your research and the group.

Regardless of role, everyone should be reachable on Slack during working hours and responsive within a business day.

## Meetings and events

### Lab meetings

We hold a regular lab meeting — typically weekly during the academic year. These rotate between research presentations (practice talks, work-in-progress), paper discussions, and skill-share sessions (e.g., a new tool, a coding technique, a methods primer). Attendance is expected for all lab members.

### One-on-ones

Each lab member meets regularly with Tamma (typically biweekly or monthly, depending on the role). These meetings are your time — come prepared with updates, questions, and anything you need help with.

### Seminars and workshops

Berkeley has an extraordinary density of seminars in economics, data science, and environmental science. Lab members are encouraged to attend talks outside their immediate area. Some recommended series include:

- ARE seminar series
- CEGA research seminars
- Berkeley Institute for Data Science (BIDS) events
- D-Lab workshops

## Time management

Research is open-ended by nature, and it can be hard to know when to stop working on something. A few principles:

- **Set weekly goals.** At the start of each week, identify 2–3 concrete deliverables you want to complete.
- **Communicate blockers early.** If you are stuck, say so. A 5-minute Slack message can save days of spinning.
- **Protect deep work time.** Block off chunks of uninterrupted time for coding, writing, and thinking. It is okay to close Slack and email during these blocks.

## Project roles

Most lab projects involve some combination of the following roles. One person may wear multiple hats, but it helps to be explicit about who is responsible for what:

- **PI**: Sets the research direction, provides feedback, secures funding.
- **Project lead**: Day-to-day ownership of the project — managing timelines, coordinating across collaborators, and making sure things move forward.
- **Analyst / data scientist**: Builds and maintains the computational pipeline, runs analyses, produces figures and tables.
- **Research assistant**: Supports data collection, literature review, and specific analytic tasks.
